coredata.dc:description: The increasing complexity of global food networks and the possible hazards of fraud, foodborne diseases, and environmental damage highlight the need for strong food traceability systems. Nanoscale carbonaceous materials known as ‘carbon dots’ (CDs), have attracted interest due to their special optical qualities, biocompatibility, and capacity for surface functionalization. Due to these characteristics, Carbon Dots are perfect candidates for the creation of nanosensors that can track and identify indications at every stage of the manufacturing and distribution of food. Innovative solutions are required since conventional systems frequently fail to provide comprehensive and real-time information throughout the food supply chain. There are many different uses for Carbon Dots-based nanosensors in food traceability applications. These nanosensors can be designed to track the origins of food items, detect viruses, identify counterfeit goods, and monitor storage conditions. They are a vital tool for guaranteeing the security, authenticity, and quality of the food supply because of their great sensitivity, selectivity, and quick reaction. The overall objective of sustainable development is in line with the ecologically friendly characteristics of Carbon Dots. Various types of Carbon Dots and their working mechanism has been described in this review.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increasing complexity of global food networks and the possible hazards of fraud, foodborne diseases, and environmental damage highlight the need for strong food traceability systems. Nanoscale carbonaceous materials known as ‘carbon dots’ (CDs), have attracted interest due to their special optical qualities, biocompatibility, and capacity for surface functionalization. Due to these characteristics, Carbon Dots are perfect candidates for the creation of nanosensors that can track and identify indications at every stage of the manufacturing and distribution of food. Innovative solutions are required since conventional systems frequently fail to provide comprehensive and real-time information throughout the food supply chain. There are many different uses for Carbon Dots-based nanosensors in food traceability applications. These nanosensors can be designed to track the origins of food items, detect viruses, identify counterfeit goods, and monitor storage conditions. They are a vital tool for guaranteeing the security, authenticity, and quality of the food supply because of their great sensitivity, selectivity, and quick reaction. The overall objective of sustainable development is in line with the ecologically friendly characteristics of Carbon Dots. Various types of Carbon Dots and their working mechanism has been described in this review.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Ensuring the safety and sustainability of our food supply in the rapidly evolving global food systems of today calls for innovative solutions (Mc Carthy et al., 2018). This groundbreaking journey is being driven by cutting-edge technology called carbon dots (CDs) and nanosensors, which have caused a paradigm shift in the way we think about food traceability. According to Jorns and Pappas (2021), carbon dots (CDs) are a collection of nanomaterials with varying structural variations that may be arranged in a multitude of ways and have distinct optical characteristics. CD nanoparticles, which are generally made of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and hydrogen, have a diameter of less than 10 nm. The most distinguishing feature of CDs is their intense, regulated fluorescence over the visible spectrum, which violates the common understanding that carbon is a dark, non-luminous substance. Other advantages of its luminescence include cheap cost, biocompatibility, high water solubility, non-toxicity, ease of synthesis, exceptional sensitivity to external environment, and strong electron donating and receiving capabilities (Manzoor et al., 2023). CDs may be used in a variety of applications, from sensing to optoelectronics, due to their unique collection of features. Our interconnected food supply chain is growing increasingly sophisticated, and mastering it is critical. Food traceability is critical for supporting sustainable practices as well as protecting against foodborne infections (Kowalska & Manning, 2022). With an emphasis on lowering the environmental impact of food production and distribution, this study examines the multifaceted value of traceability, including its role in mitigating health problems (Onyeaka et al., 2023). Food traceability refers to an extensive system that carefully monitors food items at every step of manufacture, distribution, and sale, starting from the point of origin. Important information including the suppliers of ingredients, the processing techniques, and the routes taken for shipment must be included. Traceability helps sustainable practices by maximising supply chain efficiency and enabling customers to make informed decisions by fostering accountability and transparency. Additionally, it is essential for quickly recognising and responding to foodborne disease outbreaks and contamination incidents, protecting the public's health and enhancing consumer trust in the security and integrity of the food supply (Curto & Gaspar, 2021).

Carbon Dots (CDs) are taking the spotlight in the realm of nanotechnology (Zhang, You, et al., 2021). These nanoscale carbon particles, with their individual optical and electrical properties, serve as versatile agents for molecular-level tagging. When these technologies are paired with Nanosensors, they form a dynamic duo that can provide real-time data on a wide variety of elements crucial to the integrity of our food supply chain (Osmólska et al., 2022). The synthesis of CDs and Nanosensors provides a tremendous step forward in precision, enabling hitherto unimaginable degrees of traceability. Integration of CDs with Nanosensors is a great example of progress in an age where accuracy and speed are crucial (Kulkarni et al., 2022). The once-common labor-intensive, error-prone manual traceability approaches are being replaced with an automated and simpler approach. In addition to increasing traceability accuracy, this paper investigates how CDs and nanosensors may shorten response times to potential food safety hazards. These technologies, which are pushing the limits of what is feasible, are ushering in a new era of dynamic, efficient, and responsive food traceability (Chin & Lin, 2022). The major purpose of this review is to unravel the tangled web of Carbon Dots and Nanosensors in the context of food traceability. By providing a comprehensive assessment of their roles and effects, this research intends to not only inform but also inspire a broader dialog about the revolutionary potential of these technologies.

Carbon dots, which are nanometer-sized carbon nanoparticles, signify a new era in the food industry. Due to its special qualities, such as size flexibility and photoluminescent properties, carbon dots are revolutionizing food additives and packaging (Zhao et al., 2023). They contribute flavor and color enhancement and offer novel sensory experiences; they are safe and imaginative additions. Food packaging is made more shelf-life and allows for real-time quality monitoring with the addition of carbon dots, which also give it antibacterial properties (Liu et al., 2019). Their use in food safety involves the rapid and sensitive detection of contaminants, which enhances overall safety standards, while their inclusion into nanosensors improves quality control procedures. Carbon dots are also useful in supply chain traceability, product authenticity, and food fraud detection. As part of a commitment to sustainability, carbon dots help to eco-friendly packaging solutions by actively maintaining freshness while reducing environmental consequences (Peerzada et al., 2023). The future holds more research, resolving safety concerns, and working together to realize the full potential of carbon dots for a more innovative and sustainable food industry (Wareing et al., 2021).

Carbon dot (CD) photoluminescence and electrical properties are controlled by electronic transitions across the band gap of sp2 domains and are highly influenced by surface states, fluorophores, and element doping (Ding, Zhou, et al., 2020). Quantum confinement effects (QCE) are significant, particularly in CDs with crystalline lattices, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), when the CD size is less than the Bohr radius. When the system dimensions are fewer than the exciton's Bohr radius, quantum confinement occurs, affecting properties such as band gap and relaxation dynamics. Different energy levels are produced by this confinement, which limits electron mobility in all directions. Large conjugated sp2 domains and fewer surface states in CDs are associated with higher QCE, which causes a size-dependent increase in band gap and changes photoelectron properties (Yan et al., 2019). The energy gradient between the lowest conduction band and the highest valence band increases with size, requiring more energy for excitation. This affects the efficiency of solar systems that depend on the excitons produced. The capacity of CDs, which are known for their robust carrier-carrier interactions, to produce many excitons is essential for boosting solar efficiency (Wu et al., 2019).

Carbon dots (CDs) have numerous important benefits that make their electrochemical properties make them excellent candidates in a variety of electrochemical applications (Cheng et al., 2021). The benefits of these low-cost, low-toxicity materials include rich surface functions including carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups, and their vast surface area, which promotes electrical conductivity through electron-hole pair production (Liu et al., 2020). These features make it possible to adjust the chemical and physical characteristics as well as modify the surface for improved particle stability. Furthermore, by encouraging intramolecular charge transfer and supplying reactive sites for electrocatalysis, heteroatom doping improves electronic characteristics (Tao et al., 2020). The electrochemical characteristics of CDs are governed by their size, functions, and heteroatom doping. Research indicates that smaller graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have higher specific capacitance, which makes them effective for microelectronics applications requiring high power responsiveness. By adding GQDs to activated carbon derived from husks, for example, CD modification in electrodes enhances electrochemical characteristics, decreasing charge transfer resistance and raising electrical conductivity and cycle stability. The use of CDs in supercapacitors has been proven by altering Carbon Felt electrodes with nitrogen and oxygen co-doped CDs, resulting in improved capacitance due to functionalities, heteroatom doping, and large surface area (Pathaare et al., 2023). Studies on the effect of dopant concentration on CDs show that increasing nitrogen content improves emissive and electrochemical characteristics, pushing oxidation potentials to negative values. This body of research emphasizes CDs' flexible and potential significance in improving electrochemical applications (Shen, Zhu, Yang, & Li, 2012).

Carbon-based nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10 nm that possess inherent fluorescence qualities are called carbon dots, or CDs. These were created using a variety of techniques. They consist of GQDs with graphene layers and edge functions and CNDs split into CNPs with spherical amorphous forms and CQDs with crystal lattices (Verma et al., 2021). Oxygen and nitrogen-containing surface functions are shared by all CDs. They may be better understood in terms of their size, shape, structure, and crystallinity by characterization methods such as Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM), and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM). TEM, HRTEM, AFM, Raman spectroscopy, and X-ray Diffraction (XRD). For instance, according to Kundu and Patra (2017), GQDs have a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm and a size distribution of 2.2 ± 0.2 nm. AFM sheds light on the structure, shape, and number of graphene layers. Crystallinity with a significant ID/IG ratio is shown by Raman spectroscopy. Because oxygen-containing functionalities cause disturbance in CQDs, XRD can identify it (Buatong et al., 2023). Due to their abundance of oxygen functionalities, such as carboxylic acid, CDs are easily soluble and functionalized in water. It is feasible to dope for desired qualities with heteroatoms like nitrogen. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR), and elemental analysis are used to investigate chemical functions (Wu, Liu, & Yuan, 2017a, 2017b).

Despite structural variations, carbon dots, or CDs, have comparable optical characteristics. Their chemical structure may be studied with the use of UV–vis spectroscopy (Yu et al., 2021). The peak of graphene oxide (GO) occurs at 230–235 nm, whereas in decreased GO, it shifts to 265–270 nm. CDs exhibit peaks corresponding to core and n→π∗ transitions between 200 and 280 nm and 280 and 400 nm, respectively. The peak of citric acid-based GQDs is located at 330 nm, and when nitrogen is added, it shifts to 315 nm. The edge states of GO-based GQDs are affected by the solvent used during synthesis (Smith et al., 2019). The UV–vis spectra of GQDs show transitions that vary with functionalities and edge states, both below and above 300 nm. Thiourea-induced sulfur doping in GQDs modifies absorbance at 550 and 595 nm. Red shifts in UV–vis spectra are caused by the size of CD particles (Mohamed et al., 2018). The peaks of glucose-derived GQDs at 228 and 282 nm exhibit no variation in intensity with increasing size. Changes in CDs' UV–vis spectra are caused by changes in their electronic structure and composition (Wei et al., 2020).

Carbon dots (CDs) exhibit exceptional photoluminescent (PL) qualities, providing a range of applications. The optical properties of CDs are impacted by several aspects, including material composition, shape, and synthesis techniques (Zhang et al., 2020). Still lacking, nevertheless, is knowledge of how differences in CD size and heterogeneity precisely affect their optical properties, especially concerning light excitation characteristics. Notable advancements have been made in spite of obstacles in managing the synthesis of CD and comprehending the processes of PL, especially in the context of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) and Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs). The quantum confinement effect (QCE) and surface states, which are affected by elements such as heteroatom doping, edge configurations, functionality, and defects, are the basis for the photoluminescence (PL) of these CDs (Alaghmandfard et al., 2021). It is noteworthy that CDs frequently produce blue and green light and show excitation-dependent characteristics due to size and composition heterogeneity. Recent research endeavors have focused on manipulating CD synthesis techniques to produce particles with varying color emissions by adjusting parameters such as size, surface states, and environmental conditions like solvents, pH, and concentration. This innovation holds significant potential for applications in bioimaging, LEDs, and sensors (Wu et al., 2017a, 2017b). However, gaining deeper insights into how variations in CD size and heterogeneity precisely influence their optical properties, particularly in terms of light excitation characteristics, is crucial for further advancing their utility in these diverse applications.

The photoluminescent (PL) features of CDs are influenced by the Quantum Confinement Effect (QCE), which modifies the electron population and electronic states in the core of the particle, especially in GQDs and CQDs (Langer et al., 2021). Like inorganic quantum dots, CDs typically show a red shift in emission as their size grows because of the decreased band gap brought on by QCE. It is difficult to comprehend the PL mechanism of CDs because of their diverse content and structure. These features may be studied with the use of molecular modelling, such as Density Functional Theory (DFT) (Shaikh et al., 2020). DFT studies, for example, indicated that the band gap in GQDs decreased with increasing size. CDs' PL characteristics are also affected by parameters like as size, shape, and edge layout. According to research, GQDs with zigzag edges indicate a red shift in emission with increasing size, whereas armchair edges have a greater band gap. The solvents employed in synthesis affect the sp2 conjugation length of CDs. CNDs with orange emission, for example, were made using citric acid and urea in a solvent named DMF (Vallan & Imahori, 2022). Different solvents and their ratios can result in multi-color CDs with varied sp2 domains. The effect of fluorine doping, pH, and size on PL characteristics is also discussed in the reports. However, due to the variability of CDs and their dependency on different parameters, PL characteristics are not always regulated by QCE. Because of surface and defect states, CDs, for example, may show unpredictable band gap behavior (Mahmood et al., 2018).

Carbon Dots (CDs) are composed of carbon domains with varying structures, including sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbons, as well as functional groups such as CO, C–O–C, C–O, and COOH (Lin, Sun, et al., 2018). The surface of CDs includes two major aspects: surface configurations (functionalities, edge states, defects) and heteroatom doping, which entails adding various elements like as nitrogen, sulfur, and fluorine into the conjugated aromatic domains. The photoluminescence (PL) of CDs is caused by the interaction of surface states with intrinsic core state emissions (Pschunder et al., 2019). Surface modification is essential in designing CDs to have certain qualities including stability, reactivity, and selectivity. Many studies have shown that surface passivation or modification of CDs can improve PL characteristics (Nemani et al., 2018).

Carbon Dots (CDs) often include oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, epoxy, and carboxylic acid (Mansuriya & Altintas, 2021). These groups combine to generate emissive state traps known as defects or surface states. Multi-coloured emissive CDs have attracted interest for applications in optoelectronics and bioimaging. CD emission characteristics may be modified by changing surface emissive traps, as demonstrated by Tang et al. and Ding et al., where emission changes were related to enhanced oxygen functionality rather than particle size (Ding, Zhou, et al., 2020). The addition of tartaric acid and different reaction temperatures were demonstrated to alter CD emission colors. Thiol, amine, and alcohol functionalities can easily modify PL characteristics, with amine functionalities frequently utilised because to their large quantum yields. Amino functionalization caused emission red shifts due to charge transfer between the core and surface functionalities (Kundelev et al., 2019). Excitation-dependent emission is typical, however recent work used response time and temperature modifications to generate excitation-independent CDs with dual emission hues. Surface modification with various amino functionalities and post-synthesis alterations with functionalities such as CO and CN have proven efficient in modulating emission characteristics. To generate CDs with desired emission qualities, the variance in electransitions via different surface states may be regulated (Fu et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect heteroatom doping of CDs on PL
ce:section.ce:para: Researchers frequently employ a process called heteroatom doping to adjust the optical characteristics of carbon dots (CDs) (Ding, Zhou, et al., 2020). This entails adding various components to the CD structure in order to modify band gaps, acquire particular qualities, and improve fluorescence for a range of uses. Nitrogen is a commonly utilised element for doping among other others. Peng et al. produced four kinds of CDs utilizing dopamine or 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine as a nitrogen dopant, and glucose or citric acid as a precursor. The final CDs' optical, photovoltaic, and pH sensitivity characteristics were strongly impacted by the nitrogen doping source selection (Choi et al., 2018). CDs produced with tridecanediamine, for example, had a higher quantum yield, but those synthesized with dopamine had a higher pH sensitivity. By separating fractions depending on surface charge, multicolored CDs were created, resulting in a red shift in emission due to increased graphitic nitrogen concentration. Other elements, including as sulfur, fluorine, and boron, are also used to improve the performance of CDs. For example, sulfur doping increases graphitic nitrogen concentration, resulting in red changes from green to yellow emission. Fluorine doping may cause large red shifts, as seen by CDs that transition from green to red fluorescence (Gao, Zhang, Liu, et al., 2020). Although it is difficult to make CDs with Ultralong Room Temperature Phosphorescence (URTP), nitrogen doping is essential for attaining URTP through intersystem crossover and n→π* transitions (Gao, Zhang, Liu, et al., 2020). There have also been experiments with dual doping using fluorine and nitrogen, which led to enhanced π conjugation length and emission red shifts. CDs are frequently modified to avoid aggregation-induced quenching in applications such as LEDs and bioimaging. For example, thiourea and salicylic acid-doped CDs have excellent quantum yields and greenish yellow luminosity, which makes them good candidates for LEDs. CDs with URTP features are valuable in sensing, optoelectronics, and anticounterfeiting applications, particularly when doped with boron and halides, displaying outstanding URTP with prolonged afterglow (Li et al., 2023). Fluorine-doped CDs are less frequent, yet their production can result in large red shifts in emission. Dual doping with nitrogen and fluorine can improve the characteristics of CDs, leading in emission red shifts owing to enhanced conjugation duration. Overall, heteroatom doping, particularly with nitrogen, sulfur, fluorine, and boron, demonstrates to be a flexible tool for customizing the optical characteristics of CDs for a wide range of applications (Zhou et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Core and surface configurations-induced PL of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon Dots (CDs) are composed of a carbon core with various surface characteristics. Scientists revealed that the band gap of CDs, or the energy difference between their highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals, is affected by the length of the sp2 conjugation or size (Liu et al., 2017). Researchers alter the functional groups of CDs and apply heteroatom doping to tailor their fluorescence activity. In one work, CDs emitting different hues were generated by varying the reaction temperature and reactant ratios to change the sp2 conjugation length and functionality. These CDs were integrated into epoxy resins while retaining their color emissions, with potential uses in LEDs (Zhang et al., 2022). A different study examined Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) that were produced by a hydrothermal process. These CQDs showed evidence of electron transitions in both the surface and core states, which allowed for the adjustment of PL (Photoluminescence) characteristics. Column chromatography has historically been used to separate multi-coloured CDs; however, this is a laborious procedure that results in product loss (Huo et al., 2020). By changing the reaction conditions, researchers are now investigating direct synthesis techniques. For example, changing the precursor ratio in a one-pot hydrothermal process produced multi-coloured CDs, where the oxidation degree and sp2 conjugation caused the emission hue to vary from blue to red (Singh et al., 2022).The emission characteristics of CDs can be further adjusted by combining the methods of heteroatom doping and core manipulation (sp2 conjugation). Li et al. (2021) produced CDs exhibiting near-infrared (NIR) emission and high QY in water, without requiring an external stimulus. The band gaps of surface states are changed by nitrogen doping and particle size, which causes the NIR emission. Unfortunately, poor water dispersibility limits the application of many colorful CDs made with organic solvents in watery conditions. In response, scientists have created CDs that emit green, yellow, and red light by varying the aqueous reaction solution's pH. According to Hola et al. (2017), nitrogen doping and efficient sp2 conjugation are responsible for this change in emission color, which suggests a variety of possible uses. The efficacy of light harvesting in CDs can be influenced by the spatial arrangement of chromophores. Drawing inspiration from the natural world, scientists adorned CDs with iron to maximize the absorption of visible light and boost emission efficiency. This method illustrates the possibility for effective light harvesting by imitating the spatial structure seen in natural systems (Kundu & Patra, 2017). Scholars are presently investigating several approaches to customize the emission characteristics of CDs for uses, taking into account elements like heteroatom doping, surface functions, and core size.

Carbon Dots (CDs) can be fine-tuned in their emission range and quantum yield (QY) by making simple adjustments like changing the concentration (Zhi et al., 2019). For instance, in N-doped CDs, the emission shifted from blue to red as the concentration increased. This shift was caused by self-absorption, which is caused by the closer packing of CDs at higher concentrations. Another interesting effect is seen in CDs in aqueous solution, where the emission changes from blue to green with concentration but turns yellow in a solid state. It is important to understand how CDs behave when they aggregate, as studies show that aggregation can result in quenching, enhancement, or even multicolored emissions. When CDs were subjected to copper ions, they displayed aggregation-induced emission amplification, resulting in a color shift from pale yellow to dark yellow (Lv et al., 2019). Other research has shown that aggregation-induced emission may produce a wide spectrum of hues, from red to orange and even yellow, depending on the degree of aggregation. The mechanism behind these alterations refers to interactions of surface functions and the development of new energy levels in aggregates. Because of the stacking of their carbon cores, hydrophobic CDs, for example, emitted blue when scattered and red when aggregated (Yang et al., 2019). The reversible nature of this emission change in different solvents offers adaptability. Recent study introduces zirconium-doped CDs (ZrCDs) with novel features. Because of aggregation, normal CDs displayed a red shift in emission with increasing concentration; however, Zr CDs prevented aggregation by maintaining a constant band gap. According to Chen et al. (2018a, 2018b), self-absorption effects are responsible for the concentration-dependent emission observed in normal CDs.In yet another intriguing case, hydrophilic CDs showed blue shift at alkaline pH and yellow emission when aggregated at neutral pH. According to Nayak et al. (2021), the pH-induced emissions were irreversible in alkaline circumstances (blue) but reversible in acidic settings (yellow). These results highlight the fascinating potential of modifying CDs with little modifications, providing opportunities for a wide range of applications.

Carbon Dots (CDs) synthesis has been approached in the past decade using a variety of methods that may be roughly divided into “top-down" and “bottom-up" approaches to meet the needs of individual applications. Ultrasonic synthesis and chemical exfoliation are examples of top-down approaches that include severing macroscopic carbon structures (Kadian et al., 2021). Fig. 1 shows the synthesis of CDs.

Mass manufacturing can benefit from these techniques, but they sometimes need for expensive equipment and protracted processing times. In contrast, bottom-up approaches offer more controllability by using chemical precursors like glucose to make 3D nanoparticles like Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) and Carbon Polymer Dots (CPDs) (Yang, Li, et al., 2023) (Table 1 ).

Chemical exfoliation is a simple procedure for mass-producing high-quality Carbon Dots (CDs) that does not require sophisticated equipment. This procedure uses strong acids or oxidizing agents to cleave precursor carbon materials such carbon fibres, graphene oxide, and carbon nanotubes (Brisebois & Siaj, 2020). Notably, in 2007, Mao and colleagues proved the capability of chemical exfoliation by producing bright Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) from candle soot using HNO3. Following that, Peng et al. chemically exfoliated carbon fibres using H2SO4 and HNO3, resulting in GQDs with varied photoemission colors depending on GQD size (Pho, 2023). GQD precursors such as coal and asphaltene, which are low-cost materials, have been studied; nevertheless, purification challenges and higher synthesis costs remain barriers. New technologies, such as the acid vapor cutting approach for fluorinated GQDs and the synthesis of N-doped GQDs from green tea leaf waste, have unique uses, including facile product recovery and sensing. Citric acid is also often employed in the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs); for example, it is utilised as a coordinating solvent to create high-yield CQDs (Tajik et al., 2020). Sulfuric and phosphoric acid are utilised to produce CQDs from muskmelon fruit, demonstrating the impact of peripheral moieties on PL emission. Strong oxidants such as hydrogen peroxide, KMnO4, H2SO4, and others have been utilised alongside strong acids. Kailasa et al., for example, used H2SO4 to oxidise tomato, resulting in fluorescent GQDs with low Fe3+ ion detection limits. Although acid-free approaches such as GO oxidation with KMnO4 may be used to synthesise GQDs with high efficiency, the purification procedures can be complex (Nair et al., 2017). On the other hand, using moderate oxidants, such as hydrogen peroxide, is a simpler and more environmentally friendly technique for GQD production that may be employed in bioimaging.

One distinct and interesting method for synthesizing Carbon Dots (CDs) is laser ablation, which has the benefit of simplicity and a fast-processing time. The production of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from graphite by laser ablation was first accomplished by Sun et al. (Chen et al., 2018a, 2018b). Li et al. demonstrated with laser passivation of carbon particles visible, stable, and adjustable PL performance in GQDs (John et al., 2021). Hu and colleagues presented a simple technique to create fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by laser ablation of carbon powders in an organic solvent. This process enables the surface to be modified using suitable solvents to provide adjustable photoluminescence (PL) emission (Yogesh et al., 2021). Kang presented a quick method for creating GQDs from multi-wall carbon nanotubes that show a high quantum yield of 12% and steady, blue PL emission, making them appropriate for use in optoelectronic applications. Nitrogen-doped Carbon Quantum Dots (NM-CQDs) were prepared from carbonized Platanus biomass by Ren and Fan (2023) via laser ablation, exhibiting dual-wavelength photoluminescence. Ren's team was able to achieve even more efficiency by using an ultrafast technique for homogenous CQD manufacturing that used dual-beam pulsed laser ablation (Yang, Li, et al., 2023).

The ultrasonic-assisted approach is well-known for its low cost and ease of preparation of Carbon Dots (CDs) (Mojtahedi et al., 2023). In this process, alternating high-pressure and low-pressure waves create cavitation bubbles, which develop and collapse into tiny bubbles that shear macroscopic carbon materials into nanoscale CDs. Researchers may tune CD characteristics by varying ultrasonic power, reaction time, and the carbon source-to-solvent ratio. Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) were created by ultrasonic exfoliation of graphene, which Zhuo et al. pioneered. Subsequent experiments in both aqueous and organic solvents used ultrasonic treatment to make GQDs from diverse carbon sources, including graphite, multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), graphene oxide (GO), and carbon fiberFor example, Song and colleagues demonstrated blue luminous emission by generating GQDs from an aqueous graphite dispersion (Chung et al., 2021). Heteroatom-doped GQDs are another example of how adaptable the ultrasonic method is. Chlorinated carbon fiber was subjected to direct ultrasonic exfoliation by Zhao et al. to create chlorine-doped GQDs. Furthermore, water-soluble CDs have been created from carbon-containing waste, such as food waste, using ultrasonic irradiation. These CDs have strong photoluminescent qualities, minimal cytotoxicity, and high photostability for in vitro bioimaging (Jing et al., 2023).

Top-down approaches to producing carbon dots offer distinct advantages and disadvantages, each tailored to specific applications and operational requirements. Mechanical exfoliation, ultrasound treatment, and laser-based methods represent key techniques in this realm, each with its unique characteristics and preferred applications. Mechanical exfoliation involves physically breaking down bulk carbon materials into smaller components, offering simplicity and scalability. This method is advantageous for its low-cost operation and minimal chemical involvement, making it suitable for large-scale production. However, it may yield variable sizes and shapes of carbon dots, limiting precise control over properties (Prekodravac et al., 2021). Ultrasound treatment utilizes ultrasonic waves to disintegrate carbon precursors into nanoscale fragments, providing enhanced control over particle size and morphology. This technique offers versatility in tuning parameters such as frequency and intensity to achieve desired outcomes. Ultrasound-based methods excel in producing uniform and well-defined carbon dots, making them ideal for applications requiring precise control over optical and electronic properties (Singla & Sit, 2021). Laser treatments involve irradiating carbon materials with high-energy laser beams to induce fragmentation and carbon dot formation. This approach enables rapid and selective synthesis with precise spatial control, allowing for the creation of patterned arrays and tailored structures. Laser-based methods are particularly advantageous in fabricating carbon dots for advanced optoelectronic devices and biomedical applications where precise spatial control and high-resolution patterning are critical. In selecting the most suitable method, considerations such as desired particle size, shape, uniformity, and scalability play crucial roles (Le et al., 2022). Mechanical exfoliation may be preferable for large-scale production where cost-effectiveness and simplicity are paramount. Ultrasound treatment offers superior control over particle morphology and size distribution, making it suitable for applications demanding uniformity and precision. Laser treatments are ideal for applications requiring precise spatial control and high-resolution patterning, such as optoelectronic devices and biosensing platforms. Overall, a critical analysis of top-down approaches to carbon dot synthesis, considering their specific advantages and limitations, enables informed decision-making tailored to the desired application requirements and operational constraints (Han et al., 2023).

The synthesis of Carbon Dots (CDs) may be achieved quickly and economically with the help of microwave assistance (Ahmad et al., 2023). Green fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were first created by Li et al. by cleaving graphene oxide (GO) sheets with the use of a microwave to produce homogeneous heat and break epoxy moieties. Wang and colleagues demonstrated a one-pot microwave method that produced water-soluble CDs from eggshell membranes rich in protein, which showed remarkable fluorescence and simultaneous glutathione and Cu2+ detection capacities. Yao et al. presented a unique method that shows promise for drug delivery: they used a microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment of transition-metal ions and crab shells to produce stable CDs (Yao et al., 2017). CDs produced from Mangifera indica leaves using a simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal technique had good biocompatibility and photostability, and served as intracellular temperature sensors. Ren et al. created CDs from raw cashew gum using a two-step microwave heating technique, demonstrating high biocompatibility for live-cell imaging (Subramania et al., 2023). Ren et al. created N-doped GQDs with bright blue fluorescence using microwave-assisted heat, exhibiting promise in metal ion detection (Xu et al., 2021). Ricardo et al. developed a microwave-assisted synthesis process for blue-emitting CDs, discovering a novel hybrid luminescence phenomenon caused by the interaction of fluorescent impurities with CDs. These findings highlight the microwave's ability to do rapid and diversified CD synthesis, providing prospects in a variety of applications (Kumar et al., 2020).

The hydrothermal technique for Carbon Dots (CDs) synthesis is easy and successful due to its cheap cost and nontoxicity (He et al., 2022). A combination in a water solution is encapsulated with Teflon and hydrothermally reacted at high pressure and temperature in this method. Pan et al. pioneered a green method by hydrothermally converting epoxy moieties on GO sheets into blue fluorescence CDs (Karak, 2021). In addition, Zhao's group revealed a very efficient hydrothermal approach for CQD synthesis. Some investigations used oxidants such as H2O2 to accelerate hydrothermal processes, as demonstrated by Halder et al.'s application in the production of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs). Carbon sources for hydrothermal synthesis have been investigated for waste biomass, including Saccharum officinarum juice, pomelo peel, and orange juice. Selective ion detection can be facilitated by these hydrothermally produced CQDs, which also have high biocompatibility and adjustable luminescence. High PL quantum yield has been achieved in CQD synthesis with the use of plant-based sources, such as bamboo leaves. To guarantee accurate findings in CD research, Essner et al. (2021) underlined the necessity of stringent purification techniques to exclude fluorescent impurities, owing to difficulties encountered during the hydrothermal synthesis purification process.

The solvothermal method of creating carbon dots (CDs) has the benefit of being inexpensive and needing little equipment (Chen et al., 2023). Unlike the hydrothermal approach, solvents allow for high pressure and temperature reactions by substituting water in a steel autoclave within a sealed Teflon container. Green fluorescent graphene quantum dots, or GQDs, were created by Zhu et al. in dimethyl formamide with a PL quantum yield of 11% (Jain et al., 2021). Shin and associates produced high quantum yield GQDs with blue PL emission by chemically oxidizing natural carbon precursors using ozone, a moderate oxidant. Tian et al. produced blue fluorescent GQDs with a high quantum yield of 15% by using moderate H2O2 and solvothermal exfoliation of graphite (Li et al., 2017). Liu and colleagues developed an environmentally friendly, one-step solvothermal method for the low-cost synthesis of CQDs from glycol and l-ascorbic acid that shows robust green fluorescence emission for cell labeling. To create N-doped CQDs with multifunctional fluorescence capabilities, Qian et al. suggested a straightforward solvothermal method. PEG-200 was used as a precursor by Mitra et al. to create homogenous CQDs for bioimaging because of its strong photostability and low toxicity (Das et al., 2018). He and colleagues found adaptable impurities from the ethanothermal process that may hide or modify the optical characteristics of the resulting CQDs, whereas Zheng et al. manufactured full-color emitting CQDs by solvothermal techniques by altering reactant concentrations. These results demonstrate how adaptable solvothermal synthesis is for customizing CDs for a range of uses (Zhang et al., 2023).

Pyrolysis has become a powerful method for creating luminous Carbon Dots (CDs) by starting with macroscopic carbon structures. According to Lupacciani et al. (2017), this method has several benefits, including a quick reaction time, low cost, simple operation, solvent-free methods, and scalable manufacturing. For organic carbon-containing materials to be converted into CQDs at high temperatures, four primary processes—heating, dehydration, degradation, and carbonization—are essential. Using macroscopic carbon structures as precursors, pyrolysis has become a powerful method for creating luminous Carbon Dots (CDs). According to Lupacchini et al. (2017), this method has several benefits, including a fast reaction time, low cost, ease of operation, solvent-free techniques, and scalable manufacturing. The transformation of organic carbon-containing materials into carbon quantum dots (CQDs) at high temperatures depends on four primary processes: carbonization, dehydration, heating, and degradation. Sun et al. used sonication to carbonize hair fiber combined with H2SO4 in order to create N–S-CQDs with increased N and S contents. Yin et al. described a simple carbonization method that produced extremely sensitive CQDs from the inexpensive precursor of sweet red pepper. Through direct acid hydrolysis, Wee et al. produced CQDs from bovine serum albumin protein with an average particle diameter of 1.2 nm. These varied techniques demonstrate how pyrolysis may be used to create customized CD synthesis from a variety of carbon sources (Molaei, 2019).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) may be made using the well-known Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) technique, which has attracted a lot of interest lately. According to Lin, Sun, et al. (2018), the size of the product in CVD is determined by factors such as the substrate temperature, growth time, hydrogen (H2) flow rate, and carbon supply. Fan et al. used methane on a cleaned copper foil substrate to pioneer the synthesis of CQDs. The resultant CQDs were effectively constructed in a few layers, with diameters ranging from 5 to 15 nm and a thickness profile of 1–3 nm. Ding et al. used hexagonal boron nitride as a substrate for GQDs, altering gas ratios to tune their numbers (Juma et al., 2021).

Nanosensors are sophisticated analytical instruments that work at the nanoscale and have extremely high sensitivity and precision for detecting and measuring different analytes. In the food sector, nanosensors are critical for assuring food safety, authenticity, and quality control. They are designed to monitor a variety of characteristics, such as pollutants, pathogens, and environmental conditions, allowing for real-time evaluation and intervention along the food supply chain (Srivastava et al., 2018). Nanosensors are particularly useful in detecting contaminants and hazardous compounds in food items, even at extremely low concentrations. Nanosensors reduce the dangers associated with foodborne diseases and adulteration by providing speedy and precise detection capabilities, hence protecting public health and consumer confidence. Furthermore, nanosensors are used to monitor environmental factors such as temperature and humidity, hence optimising storage conditions and reducing food spoiling and waste. This constant monitoring guarantees that food items retain their freshness and quality throughout the distribution and storage operations (Aggarwal & Idrishi, 2023).

Nanosensors are classified into three types: optical, electrochemical, and biological, with each providing distinct benefits in terms of sensitivity, selectivity, and reaction time. Optical nanosensors, for example, use light-based detection methods to identify analytes, whereas electrochemical nanosensors detect electrical signals produced by chemical processes. Biological nanosensors use biological components like enzymes and antibodies to recognize particular targets. As nanosensor technology advances, it promises to raise safety standards, handle emerging issues, and provide a more dependable, efficient, and sustainable food supply chain. The use of nanosensors in food processing, packaging, and distribution improves authenticity, transparency, and traceability, leading to better consumer protection and market integrity. Nanosensors are a crucial instrument in the current food business, with exceptional capabilities in detecting and monitoring numerous parameters required to ensure food safety, quality, and integrity. Their quick reaction times, high sensitivity, and adaptability make them essential for tackling present and future food safety and security concerns (Patel, Pillai, Bhatt, & Mohammad, 2020a, 2020b).

Nanomaterials, such as nanoparticles or nanocomposites, have special optical characteristics that enable optical nanosensors to function (Ghasemi et al., 2023). When target chemicals interact with one another, these sensors can modify how much light is absorbed, emitted, or scattered. Because of their capacity to provide extremely sensitive and accurate measurements, optical nanosensors are advantageous in applications that demand precise analysis of optical signals. Because of their capacity to detect even minute variations in light characteristics, they are used in chemical sensing, bioimaging, and environmental monitoring (Reineck & Gibson, 2017).

The detection and measurement of target analytes using electrochemical nanosensors is dependent on alterations in electrical characteristics, such as voltage or current. Nanomaterials with exceptional electrical conductivity are frequently used in these sensors (Meng et al., 2019). Electrical impulses undergo observable alterations when these nanoparticles come into contact with particular chemicals. In situations where sensitivity to electrical reactions is crucial, electrochemical nanosensors are useful (Huang et al., 2021). In medical diagnostics, they are employed widely to find disease-related biomarkers and to identify contaminants in environmental samples and chemical reaction monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon Dots and Nanosensors in food traceability
ce:section.ce:para: Nanoscale technologies, particularly Carbon Dots (CDs) and Nanosensors, are changing the face of food traceability by delivering sophisticated solutions for increased safety, transparency, and efficiency across the supply chain. Recent advances in nanotechnology have transformed the food sector by creating nanosensors that function at the nanoscale and have exceptional sensitivity (Sudha et al., 2018). These nanosensors have extensive uses in a variety of fields, including food processing, functional food development, disease detection, packaging, and shelf-life extension. Nanosensors serve an important role in food safety by detecting microbial contamination, humidity fluctuations, and temperature changes in storage settings, allowing for rapid response mechanisms (Patel et al., 2020a, 2020b). They provide a variety of detection capabilities, including the identification of biological and chemical pollutants such aflatoxins and pathogens like Salmonella (Vijayakumar et al., 2023). Nanosensors may also detect preservatives, heavy metals, veterinary medications, growth-promoting compounds, steroids, and genetically engineered food (Anh et al., 2022; Behl et al., 2022; Rajpoot et al., 2021; Rehman et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2017). Nanosensors in smart food packaging help to improve food preservation by detecting spoilage, moisture, and sweeteners, which increases product acceptability and consumer satisfaction (Sun, Zhao, & Liang, 2021; Toydemir et al., 2020). They are crucial for identifying rotting substances produced during spoiling as well as gases associated with food deterioration (Singh & Kumar, 2023). Furthermore, nanosensors consisting of transition metals such as palladium, platinum, and gold are particularly effective at detecting toxins like aflatoxin B1 in milk (Rastogi et al., 2022). Agriculture also benefits from nanosensors, which detect pesticide residues on fruits and vegetables and ensure food safety (George et al., 2022). Nanosensors serve as innovative sentinels in the food sector, changing how we view, store, and consume food while assuring its safety, quality, and market acceptability (Aggarwal & Idrishi, 2023). They have paved the way for healthier, tastier meal alternatives and helped to produce novel food items based on consumer preferences (Nikoleli et al., 2018). Using nanotechnology, the food business continues to advance, tackling numerous issues and satisfying the expectations of a more health-conscious and ecologically aware consumer base (Kuang et al., 2020). Fig. 3 shows the applications of CDs in different industries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integration of Carbon Dots and Nanosensors for advanced food traceability
ce:section.ce:para: The integration of Carbon Dots (CDs) with nanosensors offers a significant leap in food traceability, providing several benefits that improve both safety and sustainability across the food supply chain. CDs, known for their unique optical and electrical capabilities, offer a revolutionary approach for molecular-level food labelling (Mansuriya & Altintas, 2020). These nanomaterials, ranging in size from 1 to 10 nm, have remarkable photoluminescent properties, making them perfect candidates for marking and tracking diverse food items. When paired with nanosensors, which are extremely sensitive devices capable of detecting and measuring minute changes in environmental factors, CDs provide real-time monitoring capabilities that are critical for guaranteeing food safety and quality (Tshabalala et al., 2022).

Nanosensors integrated with CDs enable continuous monitoring of critical factors such as temperature fluctuations, humidity levels, and the presence of contaminants or pollutants in the food environment. This real-time data acquisition allows for prompt intervention in the event of any deviations from optimal conditions, mitigating potential risks to food integrity and consumer health. Furthermore, the combination of CDs and nanosensors improves the accuracy and efficiency of food tracking systems. Food items may be marked with distinguishing markers that simplify identification and tracking across the whole supply chain by taking advantage of CDs' unique optical capabilities (Luo et al., 2020). Nanosensors, on the other hand, allow for the collection of extensive data on the circumstances under which food items are stored, transported, and distributed. This comprehensive traceability system gives stakeholders vital insights into each food item's path from manufacturing to consumption, allowing for enhanced decision-making and responsibility at all stages of the process.

Furthermore, the improved traceability afforded by the integration of CDs and nanosensors not only enhances food safety but also contributes to reducing food waste and promoting sustainability in the food industry. By accurately monitoring environmental conditions and identifying potential hazards or spoilage factors in real time, this technology enables proactive measures to be taken to prevent food loss or contamination. Also, by providing clear and verifiable information about the origin and handling of food items, greater traceability builds customer confidence and trust in the quality and safety of the food they eat. The combination of Carbon Dots with nanosensors is a revolutionary solution to food traceability, providing unparalleled levels of precision, efficiency, and transparency in monitoring and controlling the food supply chain. By using the combined capabilities of these sophisticated technologies, stakeholders may successfully protect food integrity, decrease waste, and promote sustainable behaviours, enhancing the global food system's safety and resilience.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges and solutions in deploying CDs and nanosensors for large-scale food traceability
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon Dots (CDs) and nanosensors for large-scale food traceability have both great potential and major obstacles that must be carefully considered and strategically addressed (Neethirajan et al., 2018). Several significant obstacles must be overcome in order to fully realize the potential of these sophisticated technologies and encourage their widespread adoption. One of the fundamental issues is scalability, since increasing output to satisfy the demands of the global food business presents substantial logistical and technological hurdles, particularly in maintaining constant quality and performance. Advances in nanomaterial synthesis techniques, including as continuous flow synthesis and template-assisted approaches, are critical to attaining scalable and cost-effective CD manufacture (Habibullah et al., 2021). Furthermore, resolving the high costs of nanomaterial synthesis, sensor manufacturing, and detecting technologies is critical to make CDs and nanosensors more accessible and cost-effective for large-scale food traceability applications. Research and development activities to reduce production costs and promote technological transfer to the food sector are critical in this respect (Awaysheh et al., 2021). Furthermore, standardised detection methodologies are critical for maintaining consistency, dependability, and interoperability across various CD and nanosensor applications in the food business. Industry collaboration and regulatory initiatives are required to establish standardised protocols and guidelines, define best practices, and implement quality control measures to improve the reliability and accuracy of CD and nanosensor-based traceability systems (Habibullah et al., 2021). While the combination of CDs and nanosensors has enormous promise for revolutionizing food traceability, resolving issues of scalability, cost-effectiveness, and standardisation is critical to realising its full potential. The food sector can overcome these challenges by combining efforts in research, development, cooperation, and regulation to leverage the transformational potential of CDs and nanosensors to develop a safer, more sustainable, and transparent global food supply chain.

The future of food traceability, particularly with the combination of Carbon Dots (CDs) and nanosensors, seems bright as continuing research reveals new findings and expects game-changing developments (Li et al., 2020). Current research efforts are aimed at improving the use of CDs and nanosensors in food traceability, with an emphasis on increasing sensitivity and specificity for the detection of allergens, contaminants, and other key factors through the use of new nanomaterials. The combination of machine learning and artificial intelligence approaches with nanosensor data processing is becoming increasingly popular, with the promise of faster and more accurate insights (Mao et al., 2023).

Furthermore, the study of biodegradable and sustainable nanomaterials is consistent with the food industry's increasing emphasis on environmental sustainability. Future technology developments in food traceability CDs and nanosensors may result in the creation of sensors with greater accuracy and lower production costs. The growing integration of nanosensors into smart packaging materials is set to give consumers and supply chain stakeholders with real-time information on food goods, hence boosting transparency and trust (Chelliah et al., 2021). Furthermore, advances in connection and data analytics point to increasingly advanced traceability systems capable of delivering full insights into the whole food lifecycle. Despite the positive outlook, obstacles persist. The scalability and cost-effectiveness of CDs and nanosensors for large-scale deployment is critical. Ongoing study is necessary to address possible toxicity and environmental impact concerns connected with specific nanomaterials, while ongoing attention to ethical factors such as privacy and data security is critical (Romero-Franco et al., 2017). Furthermore, the creation of standardised protocols and regulatory frameworks to guide the appropriate implementation of these technologies is critical. Nonetheless, the trajectory indicates to a future in which nanosensors play a critical role in assuring the safety, authenticity, and sustainability of our food supply, representing a huge step towards a more transparent and technologically sophisticated food sector.

The assessment concludes by highlighting how revolutionary Carbon Dots (CDs) and nanosensors are boosting food traceability for sustainability and safety. Discs with special qualities and a variety of nanosensors allow for accurate monitoring, from contaminant identification to shelf-life evaluation. There is potential for a more effective traceability system with the combination of CDs and nanosensors. To sum up, the incorporation of Carbon Dots (CDs) with nanosensors is demonstrating remarkable progress in food traceability, which in turn is greatly augmenting sustainability and safety in the food chain. Together with the adaptability of nanosensors, carbon dots' distinct optical and chemical characteristics are bringing in a new era of accuracy and precision in monitoring several facets of food production, distribution, and consumption. The combination of Carbon Dots with nanosensors has the potential to transform the present food traceability system by offering a more effective and accurate way to monitor the whole supply chain. From identifying pollutants at the source to assessing shelf life throughout distribution, these technologies offer unprecedented transparency and precision. However, despite the hopeful advances, issues such as scalability and ethical implications must be addressed. The capacity of these technologies to scale to satisfy the demands of large-scale food production and delivery remains vital to their general acceptance. Furthermore, ethical questions surrounding the use of nanotechnology in food must be thoroughly examined to assure customer acceptability and regulatory compliance. Future research trends point to technical improvements, but a balanced approach that takes the environment's influence into account is essential. All things considered, these technologies have enormous potential to transform the food sector and guarantee a food supply chain that is safer, more transparent, and more sustainable.
Zaryab Shafi: Writing – original draft. Vinay Kumar Pandey: Conceptualization. Rahul Singh: Project administration. Sarvesh Rustagi: Formal analysis.
